medications.indication_rationale: Short-term relief of severe turbinate congestion to restore nasal airway and sleep; source used oxymetazoline 0.05%, 2 sprays/nostril BD, strictly for a MAXIMUM of 3 days to avoid rebound congestion.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 0.05% 2 sprays per nostril twice daily for a MAXIMUM of 3 days (as per source note)
contraindications_in_this_case: Do not exceed 3 days — prolonged use causes rhinitis medicamentosa (rebound congestion)

medications.indication_rationale: Relieves malaise and discomfort; source advises 1000mg four times daily as needed.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 1000mg orally up to four times daily as needed (max 4g/day) (as per source note)

medications.indication_rationale: Non-pharmacological congestion relief with no rebound risk; can be used freely.
Uncomplicated viral URTI at day 3 — antibiotics are not indicated.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Sleep with the head elevated on an extra pillow; use a cool-mist humidifier.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Head elevation and humidification reduce congestion and mouth-breathing dryness, improving sleep.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use the medicated decongestant spray for no more than 3 days; blow the nose gently before use and aim the nozzle away from the septum.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Prevents rebound congestion and reduces septal trauma/epistaxis.

patient_education_points.point: This is a common cold — a viral infection. It usually peaks around days 3–5 and settles over 7–10 days; a dry cough can linger for a couple of weeks. Antibiotics won't help.

The blockage is swollen tissue, not just mucus. The medicated spray shrinks it quickly but must be used for no more than 3 days, or the congestion rebounds worse. Saline spray and a humidifier can be used freely.

Seek care if you become genuinely short of breath at rest, wheeze, get chest pain, cough up discoloured or bloody phlegm, develop severe one-sided facial pain or symptoms lasting beyond 10 days, or a stiff neck with severe headache — go to ED/000 for breathing difficulty or neck stiffness.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Resolution; reassess for bacterial sinusitis if >10 days or double-sickening; chest review if respiratory features develop.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: True breathlessness at rest, wheeze, chest pain, high fever with stiff neck, or severe unilateral facial pain — seek urgent care.

must_include_items: Identify a self-limiting viral cold; no antibiotics
Strict <=3-day limit on topical decongestant (rebound-congestion warning)
Respiratory safety-net (rest dyspnoea, wheeze, chest pain, purulent/bloody sputum)
Sinusitis/meningism safety-net (>10 days or severe facial pain; stiff neck/headache)
acceptable_alternatives: Saline, simple analgesia, steam/humidification all acceptable supportive measures
Self-directed follow-up or GP review both acceptable
errors_of_omission: Failing to screen for lower-respiratory involvement before reassurance
errors_of_commission: Prescribing antibiotics for an uncomplicated cold
Recommending topical decongestant without a duration limit
Sending an uncomplicated cold with a negative respiratory screen to ED by ambulance
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires identifying the viral cold, the <=3-day decongestant limit, and a respiratory safety-net. Missing the respiratory screen given the breathing-distress framing is a critical failure.
